title: Median household income

infobox.value: Daily median income  in USD (2021 [PPP](/wiki/Purchasing_power_parity "Purchasing power parity")) | Annual growth (%) | Year

is the [income](/wiki/Income "Income") amount that divides a population into two groups, half having an income above that amount, and half having an income below that amount. The [median](/wiki/Median "Median") can be calculated for [household income](/wiki/Household_income "Household income"), [personal income](/wiki/Personal_income "Personal income") or [disposable income](/wiki/Disposable_income "Disposable income").

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

(Redirected from [Median household income](/w/index.php?title=Median_household_income&redirect=no "Median household income"))

Macroeconomic indicator

The **median income** is the [income](/wiki/Income "Income") amount that divides a population into two groups, half having an income above that amount, and half having an income below that amount. The [median](/wiki/Median "Median") can be calculated for [household income](/wiki/Household_income "Household income"), [personal income](/wiki/Personal_income "Personal income") or [disposable income](/wiki/Disposable_income "Disposable income").

## Measure

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Median_income&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Measure")]

The median income differs from the [national average salary](/wiki/National_average_salary "National average salary") or [GDP per capita](/wiki/Gross_domestic_product#GDP_per_capita "Gross domestic product") due to the [income distribution](/wiki/Income_distribution "Income distribution").[[1]](#cite_note-10.1111_roiw.12362-1) The median income is an [economic indicator](/wiki/Economic_indicator "Economic indicator") of the [standard of living](/wiki/Standard_of_living "Standard of living") for the median person in a country.[[2]](#cite_note-x744-2)[[3]](#cite_note-j139-3) In line with the [median voter theorem](/wiki/Median_voter_theorem "Median voter theorem") the median income growth is a [democratic](/wiki/Democracy "Democracy") indicator of [economic growth](/wiki/Economic_growth "Economic growth").[[4]](#cite_note-h505-4)

The measurement of income from individuals and households, which is necessary to produce statistics such as the median, can pose challenges and can yield results inconsistent with aggregate national accounts data. For example, an academic study on the Census income data claims that when correcting for underreporting, U.S. median gross household income was 15% higher in 2010.[[5]](#cite_note-5)

## Median equivalised household disposable income

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Median_income&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Median equivalised household disposable income")]

Further information: [Disposable household and per capita income](/wiki/Disposable_household_and_per_capita_income "Disposable household and per capita income")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/85/Annual_median_equivalised_disposable_income_per_person%2C_by_OECD_country.png/500px-Annual_median_equivalised_disposable_income_per_person%2C_by_OECD_country.png)](/wiki/File:Annual_median_equivalised_disposable_income_per_person,_by_OECD_country.png)

Annual median equivalised disposable income, in 2021, USD at PPP rates, by OECD country[[6]](#cite_note-y148-6)

The median equivalised household disposable income is the [median](/wiki/Median "Median") of the [disposable income](/wiki/Disposable_income "Disposable income") which is [equivalised](/wiki/Equivalisation "Equivalisation") by dividing income by the square root of household size; the square root is used to acknowledge that people sharing [accommodation](/wiki/Dwelling "Dwelling") benefit from pooling at least some of their living costs.[[7]](#cite_note-7)[[8]](#cite_note-8) The median equivalised disposable income for individual countries corrected for [purchasing power parity](/wiki/Purchasing_power_parity "Purchasing power parity") (PPP) for 2021 in [United States dollars](/wiki/United_States_dollar "United States dollar") is shown in the below table.[[6]](#cite_note-y148-6)
